I'm going to be talking about knowledge bases and knowledge bases are basically a structured databases of knowledge. And they can contain a lot of things.

But most commonly when people are talking about them, they are talking about relational knowledge bases that include things like entities, which are nodes in a graph and relations, which are edges between nodes.

And I'll talk about some examples of this in a little bit to make that a little bit more concrete. And then some of the questions that we ask about these are, how can we learn to create an expanded knowledge bases with, you know, neural network based methods.
And then the second question is how can we learn from the information in knowledge bases to improve like neural network models or use them in effective ways.
And how can we use a structured knowledge to answer questions.

So, the first thing I'd like to talk about a little bit is types of knowledge bases. And they come in several different varieties. The first one I'd like to talk about is a very classical one called word net.
Has anyone actually ever used word that before?
I see at least one person raising their hand so it's not entirely, uh, hasn't entirely disappeared. Is anyone heard of word not before?
Okay, more people.
So basically this used to be a really big thing in natural language processing. It's not so much anymore.
But I want to explain about it because I want to explain why this is maybe like less necessary to use, but actual knowledge bases are still more necessary to use.
So word net is a large database of words. And specifically what it does is each word or something they call a synth set is a node.
And then there are relationships between nodes. And the nodes can correspond to nouns.
And or verbs or adjectives. And nouns have different types of relations between them. So they have things like an is a relation. So like a hatchback is a type of car.

And they also make a part of relations where a wheel is a part of a car. And they also make distinctions between types and instances. So like Joe Biden is an instance of a president and president is the type.

Verbal relations are ordered by specificity. So like communicate is more broad than talk. So talk is, you know, generally a subclass of communicate and then whisper is generally a subclass of talk. So it's ordered in this way.
And then adjective relations are mostly antidems. So like wet and wet versus dry and other things like this.
And then we said synth sets, actually the each node is not a word, despite the name word that it's a set of words that all have the same meaning.

So you might have artifact and thing would both correspond to this node because they both mean basically the same thing. So it's like sets of seven. So this is also important when we talk about other types of knowledge bases as well.

What was this used for this was used for, for example, trying to figure out whether trying to find all the cars that were mentioned in like a date in a large set of text. So you would go through you would identify all synth sets or you would identify all words that correspond to these synth sets and then you would pick a step up and find motor car and you would know that like all of those were mentions of cars.

Another thing that started maybe before word now or even around the same time is word that was this data base called the psych.
It was a manually curated database attempting to encode all common sense knowledge.
